nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the Brahmaputra River basin of Assam and Arunachal Pradesh in northeastern India; inhabits seasonally-flooded forested wetlands, slow streams, oxbows, and pools, where fish reportedly construct burrows around tree stumps during the dry season
nativeRange.biotope: Seasonal monsoon-flooded forest pool / Slow-flowing stream

decorPreferences: tight-fitting lid with an air gap above water (mandatory — obligate air-breather and accomplished jumper)
dense floating vegetation (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, water sprite) to dim lighting and provide cover
driftwood, roots, and leaf litter for shade and tannin release
robust rooted plants such as Anubias and Java fern attached to wood
caves, root tangles, or PVC hides for retreat and territorial spacing
minimal current — still or very gentle flow preferred

diet.notes: The Rainbow Snakehead is a carnivore that feeds on insects, insect larvae, earthworms, freshwater shrimp, mussels, krill, and small fish both in the wild and in captivity. In the aquarium it readily accepts a wide range of meaty frozen and live foods including bloodworms, mosquito larvae, chopped prawn, mussel meat, and earthworms — the last of which are a particular favourite. Pellet acceptance varies by individual: some fish can be trained onto sinking carnivore pellets, while others remain reluctant. Live feeder fish are generally advised against, as they carry disease risk and may encourage live-prey fixation that makes weaning onto prepared foods more difficult. Mammalian and avian meat such as beef heart or chicken should not be fed. Feeding three to four times per week is typical; snakeheads are prone to obesity if overfed.

goodWith: conspecifics in pairs or small groups (one of the few Channa species reported to tolerate conspecific cohabitation, especially when raised together)
robust mid- and bottom-dwelling tankmates of similar size in very large tanks (large barbs, loaches, larger cyprinids)
best housed as a species tank or with a single carefully-chosen dither species
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (will be predated)
slow or long-finned fish (potential targets and fin damage)
shrimp, snails, and other invertebrates (will be eaten)
very aggressive or boisterous tankmates that monopolize feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Mirko Hartig, 670x510. Striking, sharp, well-lit lateral shot among plants showing the diagnostic blue anal-fin edge and orange head markings on a vibrant adult; only a small corner credit.
Photo by Mar11, 2604x2252 (high-res). Best shows the rainbow coloration - vivid orange caudal, blue lateral line, green-teal flank mottling; caveat: juvenile and slightly soft focus.
Photo by Melanochromis, 540x360. Clean full-body lateral view with teal-blue dorsal fin and barred/spotted flank; good ID reference angle, lower resolution and muted body color.

summary: The Rainbow Snakehead (Channa bleheri) is a dwarf snakehead from the Brahmaputra basin of northeastern India, reaching around 17–22 cm. It is among the few Channa species generally considered peaceful enough to keep in pairs or small groups. As an obligate air-breather and accomplished jumper, a sealed lid with an air gap is essential.
careNotes:
The Rainbow Snakehead (Channa bleheri) is a small, brightly-coloured snakehead described by Vierke in 1991 from the upper Brahmaputra drainage in Assam and Arunachal Pradesh, northeastern India. In the trade it is among the more popular dwarf Channa, sought after for its modest adult size, vivid orange, blue, and green flank patterning, and an unusually social disposition for the genus. A range of roughly 17–22 cm in well-kept aquaria is the most commonly cited adult size.

The Rainbow Snakehead experiences a strongly seasonal climate in the wild, with monsoon-warm summers (22–28°C) and cool winters that can drop into the mid-teens. A temperature reduction of several weeks per year (down to 16–20°C) is widely reported as important for long-term health and is generally required to trigger breeding. Year-round tropical temperatures around 26–28°C are frequently linked to shortened lifespan and poor condition. Water chemistry is forgiving — pH from mildly acidic to neutral and a broad hardness tolerance — but consistent quality and regular partial water changes are important; despite their air-breathing reputation, snakeheads are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite accumulation.

A tank with at least a 120 cm footprint and around 200 L is generally advised, even though the fish itself is modest in size — they are territorial top-dwellers that benefit from a long, well-furnished swimming area with multiple sight breaks. Like all Channa, this species is an obligate air-breather using a suprabranchial organ (an accessory breathing structure that allows the fish to take oxygen directly from the air) and must always have unobstructed access to the surface. A tightly-fitted lid with no gaps is essential because Rainbow Snakeheads jump readily and can survive considerable time out of water. An air gap of several centimeters between the water surface and the lid is generally advised to keep the air above the water warm and humid.

Temperament is the standout trait of the species: unlike most Channa, the Rainbow Snakehead is often kept as a bonded pair or small group, and is among the most commonly recommended Channa for keepers wanting a more sociable snakehead. Aggression toward conspecifics can still emerge as fish mature, particularly during breeding, so individuals should be monitored and the tank should be large and well-decorated enough to provide refuge for subdominant fish.

Legal status: All Channa species are listed as Injurious Wildlife under the US Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42); import into and interstate transport within the United States is prohibited without a federal permit. Several other jurisdictions also restrict the keeping of snakeheads. Prospective keepers must verify local and national regulations before acquisition.
breedingNotes: The Rainbow Snakehead has been bred in captivity by specialist keepers and is one of the more accessible Channa for breeding projects. Spawning is generally triggered by a cool-down period (down to roughly 16–20°C for several weeks) followed by gradual warming and increased feeding, mimicking the post-monsoon transition in the native habitat. Accounts of spawning behaviour vary — surface free-spawning embraces and cave-site egg deposition have both been reported, and the difference may reflect setup-dependent variation. Either way, parental care is active and both sexes guard the young; the parents have also been reported to provision fry with unfertilised eggs. Fry are reared on Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) and microworms, transitioning to chopped frozen foods as they grow. Separating fry from parents once they are feeding independently is generally advised.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa bleheri
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/rainbow-snakehead) returned HTTP 404. SeriouslyFish provided type-locality (Brahmaputra basin), native range (Assam/Arunachal Pradesh), water parameters with seasonal variation, adult standard length (14–17 cm SL), tank base (120×30 cm), temperament, diet, and reproductive behavior.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa bleheri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Channidae, Anabantiformes — superseding older Perciformes placement), authorship (Vierke, 1991), distribution (India), maximum total length (14.4 cm TL), and obligate-air-breather status.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Channa bleheri (Rainbow Snakehead)
additional.notes: Supplemented care notes: confirmed unusually peaceful conspecific temperament for a Channa (pairs/groups tolerated), strong seasonal temperature variation (~16–18°C winter, 22–24°C summer) is generally advised, cave-spawning observations with biparental fry care including parental egg-provisioning. Cited an upper size of ~25 cm, included to broaden the size range disagreed upon by SeriouslyFish (14–17 cm SL) and FishBase (14.4 cm TL).

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning is generally triggered by a cool-down period (16–20°C) followed by gradual warming. Accounts vary between surface free-spawning embraces and cave-site egg deposition with biparental care; both patterns have been reported and may be setup-dependent. Both parents guard eggs and fry and have been reported to provision young with unfertilised eggs. Fry require Artemia nauplii initially. Rated advanced due to the need for seasonal temperature conditioning and careful management of pair compatibility.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the US Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US without federal permit. Other jurisdictions also restrict snakehead keeping — verify locally before acquisition.
